content: Most innovation failures are framing failures, not execution failures. Teams spend enormous energy brilliantly solving the wrong problem because they skip the uncomfortable work of sitting with the question before generating solutions. The highest-leverage skill in innovation is not idea generation — it is problem definition and reframing before any solution-seeking begins.
implication: Before your next ideation session, invest at least as much time defining and reframing the problem as you plan to spend generating solutions. If the group can't articulate the problem three different ways, they aren't ready to brainstorm.
source_ref: Ideaflow: The Only Business Metric That Matters, Perry and Utley

content: The 'How Might We' prompt is not a linguistic nicety — it is a precision instrument for problem framing. The phrase 'how' assumes a solution exists; 'might' removes judgment from early possibilities; 'we' makes it a collective challenge. Crafting the right HMW statement before generating any ideas is the disciplined act that separates design thinkers from wishful thinkers.
implication: Treat your HMW statement as a hypothesis to be tested, not a sentence to be perfected. Write five to ten versions of it before picking one — the act of rewriting the question is itself the innovation work.
source_ref: AI and Design Thinking with Stanford's Jeremy Utley — Bloom Mood Podcast

content: Utley draws a sharp distinction between 'bonehead' and 'brilliant' innovation: the bonehead move is to jump immediately to solutions; the brilliant move is to realize that the presented problem is almost never the real problem. The real problem is usually one or two 'why' questions upstream from the symptom that triggered the meeting.
original_quote: The problem as stated is rarely the problem worth solving.
implication: When a team brings you a problem, make it a discipline to ask 'why is this a problem?' and 'what would solving this make possible?' at least twice before allowing solution generation to begin.
source_ref: From Bonehead to Brilliant: Innovation through Ideaflow — TD.org Accidental Trainer Podcast

content: Premature convergence is the silent killer of innovation processes. Most teams instinctively rush from problem statement to solution, skipping the expansive, uncomfortable middle ground where the real insight lives. Sitting with a question — deliberately resisting the urge to answer it — is a trainable discipline, not a personality trait.
implication: Install a structural pause between problem statement and ideation in every innovation process. Make it explicit and timed: 'We are not going to generate solutions for the next twenty minutes. We are going to reframe the question.'
source_ref: Ideaflow: The Only Business Metric That Matters, Perry and Utley

content: Generation and evaluation are cognitively incompatible modes that cannot operate simultaneously without evaluation winning every time. When teams are asked to brainstorm while implicitly judging, the strangest ideas — which are statistically the most likely to be breakthrough ideas — die before they are spoken. Protecting the generative phase from evaluative interference is the core discipline of innovation facilitation.
original_quote: Brainstorming as commonly practiced is an evaluation meeting with a permission slip.
implication: Run divergence and convergence in separate, explicitly named sessions. When you open a generative phase, announce it, set a quota, and enforce a no-evaluation rule. When you switch to evaluation, announce that too.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley on Why Mastering Ideaflow Elevates Everything Else — Passion Struck Podcast

content: The 'what does this make me think of?' reframe is Utley's signature micro-intervention for shifting a team from evaluative to generative mode. The question 'what do I think of this idea?' triggers critical judgment; the question 'what does this idea make me think of?' triggers associative thinking. This single linguistic pivot can unlock a stuck room in under a minute.
implication: Teach this one phrase to every facilitator and team lead in your organization. Post it on the wall of every meeting room where innovation work happens. It costs nothing and consistently shifts cognitive posture.
source_ref: How to Master AI Powered Creativity in Just 13 Minutes — Jeremy Utley YouTube Channel

content: Utley frames the diverge/converge distinction as meta-language that every innovation team must internalize. Most meeting dysfunction comes not from bad ideas or bad people but from mismatched cognitive modes — one person is generating while another is filtering, and neither knows it. Naming the mode explicitly — 'are we flaring or focusing right now?' — is the facilitator's most powerful single intervention.
implication: Before every meeting that involves creative work, explicitly declare which mode you are in and why. If you catch the group switching modes prematurely, name it out loud rather than letting the collision continue invisibly.
source_ref: Ideaflow: The Only Business Metric That Matters, Perry and Utley

content: Idea quotas are not motivational gimmicks — they are the structural mechanism that changes the social contract inside a brainstorm. When the goal is 'share a good idea,' every participant filters ruthlessly before speaking, and the strange ideas die in silence. When the goal is 'help us hit 50,' participants stop self-editing and the collective intelligence of the room actually becomes available.
original_quote: Quotas change the social contract from 'say something impressive' to 'help us hit the number,' and that is the only condition under which strange ideas survive.
implication: Set an explicit, uncomfortably high idea quota for every generative session — higher than seems reasonable. The discomfort of the number is a feature, not a bug; it forces the group past the obvious and into genuinely novel territory.
source_ref: Ideaflow: The Only Business Metric That Matters, Perry and Utley

content: Most organizations have a generation problem, not an evaluation problem. Companies build sophisticated portfolio frameworks, stage gates, and scoring rubrics — elegant filters applied to an impoverished idea pipeline — and then wonder why nothing breakthrough emerges. A world-class filter on a mediocre pipeline still produces mediocrity. The leverage is almost always upstream of the funnel, in the generation stage.
implication: Audit your innovation process for where the real bottleneck sits. If you have more evaluation infrastructure than generation infrastructure, you have your answer. Redirect investment upstream — toward volume, diversity, and quality of ideas generated before any selection begins.
source_ref: Ideaflow: The Only Business Metric That Matters, Perry and Utley

content: Utley draws on Stanford d.school's design thinking tradition to argue that problem framing is an iterative, not linear, process. The team's first articulation of the problem is almost always a first draft — a placeholder that will be revised, narrowed, broadened, or completely discarded as they learn more. Treating the initial problem statement as fixed is the most common design thinking mistake.
implication: Build explicit 'problem reframe' checkpoints into your innovation sprints — moments where the team is required to ask whether they are still solving the right problem before continuing. Make reframing an expected event, not a sign of failure.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley: Co-Author of Ideaflow & Director of Stanford's d.school — Kara Goldin Show

content: The Wonder Wander is Utley's physical practice for escaping the closed loop of the brain reasoning with itself. When stuck on a problem, you take the question into the world — a walk, a store, a neighborhood — and deliberately collide it with whatever you encounter. A traffic light becomes a metaphor for prioritization; a nail salon becomes a model for upselling. The world is a library of analogies for any problem you carry into it.
implication: When a team is stuck on a problem framing, send them on a twenty-minute Wonder Wander with the question written on a card. Reconvene and share what each person encountered and what it made them think about the problem. The collisions almost always produce reframes that desk-bound thinking cannot.
source_ref: Ideaflow: The Only Business Metric That Matters, Perry and Utley

content: Forced collision with random stimuli is a deliberate technique, not a happy accident. Utley argues that the brain alone with itself only recirculates what it already knows, which is why breakthroughs rarely come from sitting quietly at a desk and thinking harder. Creativity requires importing novel frames — random images, objects, domains — and asking 'how does this relate to my problem?' to trigger genuinely new connections.
implication: When facilitating ideation sessions, bring random provocations into the room: magazine cutouts, objects from a dollar store, random words from a dictionary. Force explicit connections between the provocation and the problem. The strangeness of the connection is directly proportional to the novelty of the idea it produces.
source_ref: Ideaflow: The Only Business Metric That Matters, Perry and Utley

content: Utley identifies the instruction 'think of something new' as operationally useless advice. The brain cannot execute that command because newness is not a retrievable category — it is an emergent property of unexpected collisions between existing frames. The operational replacement is 'think of connections' — specifically, connections between this problem and something apparently unrelated.
original_quote: The instruction 'think of something new' is bad advice. 'Think of connections' is operational.
implication: Replace 'think of new ideas' with 'find connections between this problem and...' in every ideation prompt you write. The more specific and surprising the second domain, the more novel the ideas that emerge.
source_ref: Stop Trying to Be Original — Jeremy Utley YouTube Channel

content: Originality, properly understood, is not the starting point of innovation — it is the outcome of combinatorial depth. Utley argues that the obsession with being original is itself an innovation block because it frames the goal as producing something from nothing, which is neurologically impossible. The more productive frame is: what two things, when combined, have not been combined before?
implication: Stop asking your teams to 'be more creative' or 'think more originally.' Instead, ask them to map the edges of their domain and identify what adjacent domains or technologies have never been connected to the problem at hand. Originality will follow.
source_ref: Stop Trying to Be Original — Jeremy Utley YouTube Channel

content: Utley consistently names 'ideaflow' as the leading indicator of innovation health — the volume and velocity of ideas per unit of time — in contrast to lagging indicators like revenue, launches, or patents. This leading/lagging distinction is not merely semantic: it determines where leaders direct attention and investment. If you want to change next year's innovation output, you must change this quarter's idea generation rate.
implication: Measure your team's ideaflow explicitly and regularly. Track how many ideas are generated per person per week in designated ideation time. Treat a declining ideaflow number as an early warning system for innovation health, not a cultural curiosity.
source_ref: Ideaflow: The Only Business Metric That Matters, Perry and Utley

content: The belief that you are 'not a creative person' is itself the primary creative block — and it operates at the organizational level as much as the individual level. When teams accept the narrative that creativity belongs to certain people or certain departments, they structurally exclude most of the organization's cognitive capacity from the innovation process. Utley treats creativity as a trainable, measurable skill, which means the narrative is falsifiable.
implication: Audit the implicit creativity narrative in your organization. If innovation is siloed in a 'creative team' or 'innovation lab,' you have a framing problem before you have a process problem. Redistribute ideation as a universal expectation, not a specialist function.
source_ref: Ideaflow: Achieving Breakthrough Creativity and Ideas — We Are Human Leaders Podcast

content: Utley draws on Stanford d.school's principle that empathy precedes ideation — you cannot frame a problem well without first understanding the human experience at its center. Teams that skip the empathy phase and proceed directly to 'How Might We' statements are framing problems through their own assumptions rather than through observed human behavior, which reliably produces solutions that solve no one's actual problem.
implication: Make direct human observation or interview a required input before any HMW statement is finalized. Even a single thirty-minute conversation with someone who lives the problem will produce more accurate framing than hours of internal discussion.
source_ref: AI and Design Thinking with Stanford's Jeremy Utley — Bloom Mood Podcast

content: Utley argues that the discomfort of being stuck is a diagnostic signal, not a problem to suppress. When a team feels blocked on a problem, that sensation is information: it signals that the current framing has exhausted its productive space and a reframe is required. Leaning into the discomfort — asking 'what would we have to believe for this problem to look completely different?' — is the correct response, not pushing harder on the existing frame.
implication: Train your teams to treat stuckness as a reframe trigger rather than a productivity problem to be overcome by more effort. When a group reports being stuck, the first intervention should always be a problem reframe exercise, not a pep talk or a deadline extension.
source_ref: You're Not Tired of AI. You're Scared of It. — Methods of the Masters Blog

content: Utley uses the 'big rocks before sand' metaphor to argue that the sequence of problem-solving activities matters as much as the activities themselves. If you fill a jar with sand first, the rocks don't fit. The 'big rocks' in innovation are the high-leverage, uncomfortable upstream activities — deep empathy, genuine problem reframing, divergent ideation — that get crowded out when teams default to the comfortable sand of execution and refinement.
implication: Sequence your innovation sprints so that the highest-leverage, most uncomfortable activities — problem reframing, empathy research, divergent ideation — happen first, while attention and energy are at their peak. Never let execution logistics eat the space that should belong to framing.
source_ref: Are You More of What You Value? — Methods of the Masters Blog

content: Utley notes that the Capgemini research context surfaced a consistent pattern: leaders who believe they understand their customers' problems without direct observation are almost always solving a proxy problem — a smoothed, averaged, assumption-laden version of the real one. The practice of going back to direct observation whenever a solution feels stuck is the structural antidote to proxy-problem solving.
implication: When a solution has been refined multiple times and still doesn't land, resist the urge to refine it further. Instead, go back to the problem definition and ask whether you are solving the problem people actually have or the problem you assumed they have.
source_ref: Creativity in the age of AI: A conversation with Jeremy Utley — Capgemini

content: For Utley, the design thinking process is not a linear pipeline but a permeable membrane — teams should be able to move back from prototyping to ideation, from ideation to reframing, and from reframing to empathy whenever new information demands it. The innovation process that cannot move backward is not a process — it is a conveyor belt, and conveyor belts produce commodities.
implication: Build explicit 'permission to regress' into your innovation process documentation. Make it clear that returning to an earlier phase when new information warrants it is intelligent process discipline, not project failure.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley: Co-Author of Ideaflow & Director of Stanford's d.school — Kara Goldin Show

content: Utley identifies a specific failure mode he calls the 'thin pipeline problem': organizations with sophisticated evaluation frameworks but weak generation practices. The solution is not a better filter — it is a dramatically higher volume of inputs entering the pipeline at the ideation stage. This requires treating idea generation as a discrete, protected, measurable activity rather than an incidental byproduct of meetings.
implication: Audit your last innovation cycle: how many distinct ideas were generated before selection began? If the number is under thirty, your evaluation infrastructure is irrelevant — the constraint is upstream. Invest in generation capacity before refining your selection criteria.
source_ref: Ideaflow: The Only Business Metric That Matters, Perry and Utley

content: Utley's research at Stanford d.school surfaces a consistent finding: the teams that produce the most breakthrough outputs are not the teams with the most talented individuals — they are the teams with the most disciplined process for protecting the generative phase from premature evaluation. Process design predicts innovation output more reliably than talent composition.
implication: Stop optimizing your innovation teams primarily through talent selection. Optimize the process first — specifically, the structural protection of divergent thinking time. A disciplined process will outperform talented individuals operating inside a broken process.
source_ref: Ideaflow: Achieving Breakthrough Creativity and Ideas — We Are Human Leaders Podcast

content: Utley uses AI as both a subject and a method for improving problem framing. He teaches practitioners to give AI the problem statement and ask it to generate five alternative framings of the same problem — not to outsource the thinking, but to stress-test their own framing assumptions. The AI's alternative framings function as a mirror that makes invisible assumptions visible.
implication: Before finalizing any problem statement for an innovation sprint, run it through an AI model with the prompt: 'Generate five alternative ways to frame this problem that would lead to fundamentally different solution spaces.' Use the output as a reframing stimulus, not a substitute for human judgment.
source_ref: Don't Use AI. Work With It. — Methods of the Masters Blog
